scenario: A logistics company built a real-time shipment tracker. Drivers call dispatch instead of using it because they say 'it takes too many taps to update a status.'
why: The tech works. Drivers reject it because the interaction design doesn't match their context (driving, gloves, rushed). Form factor and UX problem.

scenario: An insurance company's claims dashboard shows all the right data, but adjusters still maintain personal spreadsheets to track their caseload.
why: Data is correct and accessible. Adjusters don't trust the dashboard or it doesn't match their mental model. They've built a shadow system. UX research problem.

scenario: After migrating to a new HR platform, time-to-hire increased by 40% even though the new system has more automation features.
why: More features created more complexity. The automation exists but isn't surfaced in a way that matches recruiter workflows. The tool slowed people down.

scenario: A bank's mortgage application takes 3 days to process. 2.5 of those days the application sits in someone's queue because they didn't know it was their turn.
why: Process logic is fine. Handoff visibility is the problem. People don't see what's waiting for them, or notifications are buried. Workflow visibility design problem.

scenario: A hospital built a patient intake form that collects 47 fields. Nurses enter fake data in optional fields to get past validation faster during peak hours.
why: The system enforces a rigid flow that doesn't match reality. Nurses game it because the form doesn't adapt to context. Progressive disclosure and context-aware design would fix this.

scenario: A manufacturing company's quality control app flags defects accurately, but floor supervisors override 60% of the flags without investigation.
why: Detection works. Supervisors don't trust it or the cost of investigating every flag is too high relative to how alerts are presented. Better prioritization and confidence visualization would change behavior.

scenario: A retailer launched a supplier self-service portal. After 6 months, only 12% of suppliers use it. The rest still email their account manager.
why: Portal works. Suppliers prefer email because the portal is harder than sending a message. Onboarding friction, navigation complexity, or lack of trust. Adoption and UX problem.

scenario: Your company's chatbot resolves 70% of customer queries, but your NPS score dropped 15 points since launching it.
why: The chatbot works technically. NPS dropped because the experience of interacting with it is poor: forced bot-first flows, no easy human escalation, impersonal tone. Conversational UX problem.

scenario: Your fraud detection system catches 95% of fraudulent transactions. Your fraud team still reviews every flagged case manually because they don't trust it.
why: The model is excellent. The problem is how confidence scores are communicated. Explainability, clear escalation criteria, and trust-building in the interface would change adoption.

scenario: Your internal tool auto-generates reports, but managers rewrite 80% of the executive summary before sharing it.
why: The report template doesn't match how managers communicate. Understanding what they change and why is a UX research task. Redesign the output format.
